What Explains Who We Helped Each Other?

Explaining Peer Support in Learning Social Networks Theory and Methods

Adam Talsma

The Graduate Institute, Geneva

23 December 2021

  • Introduction
  • Theoretical Perspectives
  • Data sources
  • Hypotheses
  • Network Visualization and Statistics
  • Modelling
    • Modeling Who Helps: DyNAM Rate Function
    • Modeling Who Receives Helps: DyNAM Choice Function
  • Discussion of Results
  • Conclusion

Introduction

This social networks class involved online interaction among students and teachers as a key part of the learning process. We used Moodle then Slack to seek out and provide help in learning networks and R. Our teachers often helped, but we students also provided responses and helped each other. This project seeks to determine what explains who we helped and why, and considers likely network and non-network factors as possible determinants.

Theoretical Perspectives

Education scholars theorize peers as effective co-constructors of learning, by helping students learn beyond what they could on their own (Vygotsky, 1978). Empirical studies have shown that online collaboration platforms, including Slack, can support peer learning in response to individual queries among graduate students (Walker et al., 2021. Some prior studies contend that graduate students give knowledge in learning communities for four reasons: altruism, social trust, belonging (Brouwer and Jansen, 2019), and cultural conversance (Sadykova 2014). In contrast to the literature to date, I propose that the evolving structure of the digital interactions between students may also explain their decisions to help.

Data sources

For this project, I mined our class’s digital footprints in Moodle and Slack that were accessible to all of us. I built a peer support network of directed ties for each time a student helped another student. I then surveyed each of the 24 student to gather relevant network attributes, and to build a network of offline relationships at two points in time: before the class and towards the end of class.

Hypotheses

The three network hypotheses that I introduce as my explanatory variables are:

  • \(H_1\): In-degree Centrality: the more students receive help from peers, the more likely they are to “pass it on” by helping others.

  • \(H_2\): Out-degree Centrality: The more students help, the more likely they are to keep helping.

  • \(H_3\): Reciprocity: Students are more likely to return the favor by helping those students that have helped them.

I test my hypotheses with proxies for the theoretical controls that the literature has identified.

  • Altruism: Students that are more altruistic help other students more.

  • Social Trust: Students are more likely to give help to those who they have built more social trust with through one-on-one interactions.

  • Belonging: Students who have been at the Institute longer are more familiar with the environment, and so they will help more, and/or students are more likely to help those who are in the same year as them.

  • Cultural Conversance: Students coming from a different culture, will be less likely to give or receive. I consider Local/International, European/Non-European and Northern/Southern.

I also considered a few additional control variables:

  • Network transitivity: I hypothesized reciprocity might be a factor due to threads, but threads could also produce transitivity in peer support.

  • Knowledge: The requests for help were mostly questions on R programming are knowledgeable about R

  • Difference in Learning style: Perhaps some people simply prefer doing assignments in groups, as opposed to alone, which may lead them to request and provide more support.

  • Gender: It is after-all “a man’s world”, and with a male Professor, perhaps men are more likely to provide help than women, and women are more likely to receive help.

Note that I ruled out all variables that were the same across the class. For example, everyone knew that their engagement on Moodle/Slack would factor into their final grade.

Network Visualization and Statistics

The diagrams below reveal a few things:

  • The size of the nodes in Fig. 1 reveal significant variations in out-degree centrality (helping more), as well as in-degree based on the arrows.

  • Visually Europeans and Men seem to be helping more than Non-Europeans and Women.

  • These trends do not seem to correspond with Fig. 2, as we see that the outdegree centrality (socializing with more peers) as represented by the size of the nodes varies less, and many of the students that are on the fringes of Fig. 1 are centrally located inFig. 2 and vice versa.

Personal Interactions Network

Modelling

To test my hypotheses, I built a DyNAM using Goldfish. I selected DyNAMs because my data consists of events time-stamped by the minute. DyNAMs are especially relevant because it allows me to model the effects due to helping (the rate function) separately from the effects of receiving help (the choice function). The modeling was done using a backwards selection procedure. I used “window effects” to evaluate whether more recent network interactions were explaining the help network, settling on a 45 minute window providing.

Modeling Who Receives Helps: DyNAM Choice Function

Discussion of Results

The rate models show consistent results, and I settled on the 45 minute Window Model for the rate model, as it has the lowest overall AIC value. The choice model shows different explanations for the window and the other two models, so model 2 and 3 are interpreted.

Model Comparison and Selection
AIC Model 1: Full AIC Model 2: Trimmed AIC Model 3: Window
Rate Model 3719 3720 3663
Choice Model 792 755 725

With a confidence level of at least 99% we find that students that help more are more likely to keep helping (\(H_2\) confirmed), as are Europeans, Men, and people who have been helped in the last 45 minutes (\(H_1\) mixed results as it is only within this window).

With a confidence level of at least 95% we find that students selection of who to help without a window is effected by who have been receiving help generally, those they have been helping most recently (\(H_2\)), those who have helped them (support for \(H_3\)), and those who are in their year (support for belonging/familiarity). When a 45 minute window is imposed however, all of these lose significance and the same predictors that drove the rate function explain who they choose. This phenomenon is likely a function of threads, further evidence how the structure of the online interactions influences who gets help.

Interestingly, the only variable taken from the literature that had significant effect across all the models was the belonging/familiarity, and perhaps cultural convergence, given Europeans were significantly more engaged. And, if anything, altruism, knowledge of R, social interactions offline, and preference for working in groups were negatively associated with helping others.

An analysis of the waiting time reinforces these findings. Across all students the average time between helps was 42 days. For a non-European female that has helped 3 others already their time between helps drops to 29 hours. However, for a European male that has not helped anyone yet, their expected response time is only 10 hours.

Conclusion

We conclude that network effects do have significant explanatory power for peer support online, as do attributes including gender, cultural cognizance, and belonging. Suprisingly, we found that, if anything, altruism, knowledge of R, social interactions offline, preference for working in groups were negatively associated with helping others. This
